behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure boundary is entirely within the handler function's signature-verification gate. The verification is a local HMAC-SHA256 computation and constant-time comparison — no module handoff is required to expose a bug. The 'body is not forwarded' clause adds a call-count assertion on a mock queue, which is still a single-function boundary. REAL-INFRA: No OS or network needed. MOCK-HIDE: A mock queue is sufficient because the failure (wrong 401 logic or forwarding despite bad sig) lives in the handler, not in queue internals. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: Neither applies. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would be needed only if the bug could hide in a module handoff; here the gate is a local conditional in the handler.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the handler function directly with a synthetic request whose X-Signature-256 header is computed over a different body. Assert the return value is 401. Assert the mock queue's enqueue method was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is an ordering bug within the handler — the handler sends the 200 before (or instead of after) the enqueue call. This is a single function's control flow, not a module-boundary handoff. A mock queue that records the timestamp of its enqueue call, compared against when the handler resolves, is sufficient to expose the bug. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network required. MOCK-HIDE: The ordering guarantee is tested by recording call sequence; the real queue's internals are irrelevant to whether the handler orders operations correctly. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would apply if the ordering constraint required observing the queue's durable write commit, but the behavior specifies 'written to the queue' (call made), not 'durably committed on disk'.
behaviors.test_description: In the handler under test, replace the queue with a spy that records its invocation timestamp. Invoke the handler and capture when the returned promise resolves (the 200 ack). Assert the spy was called strictly before the promise resolved.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is a fire-and-forget bug — the handler may be incorrectly awaiting downstream completion before returning. This is purely a property of the handler's async control flow; it does not require multiple modules to interact to manifest. A slow mock downstream is sufficient to expose the bug. REAL-INFRA: Not required. MOCK-HIDE: A mock downstream that never resolves (or resolves after a delay) will surface any await that blocks the response. REJECTION-GATE: Integration would be needed only if the async handoff happened across a module boundary that could introduce hidden awaits; here the handler's own control flow is the sole concern.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a downstream mock whose processing promise is held open (never resolved during the test). Invoke the handler and assert the returned HTTP-response promise resolves before the downstream promise is resolved. Confirm the downstream was invoked (fire-and-forget, not skipped).

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: A single-function unit test cannot expose concurrency-related blocking bugs (e.g., an inadvertent shared lock, event-loop starvation, or a per-process connection pool bottleneck). The failure only appears when multiple requests are in-flight simultaneously and earlier slow downstream processing is actually occupying resources. This is a module-boundary interaction between the HTTP layer, the handler, the async queue write, and the downstream dispatcher. REAL-INFRA: Real wall-clock elapsed time under concurrent in-process HTTP calls (e.g., supertest or Fastify inject) is required; mocked timers cannot prove the 3-second SLA holds under real scheduling. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking concurrency away would make the test trivially pass while hiding the exact bug category. REJECTION-GATE: System (real OS port) is not required because in-process HTTP injection provides genuine concurrency without an OS-level TCP stack.
behaviors.test_description: Start the server with a downstream mock that sleeps for 10 seconds per event. Fire 5 concurrent in-process HTTP requests (e.g., via supertest). Assert that all 5 responses are received within 3 seconds of the first request being sent, using real wall-clock measurements (Date.now / performance.now).

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Idempotency is a state-across-requests property. The failure (duplicate enqueue) appears only when the handler correctly reads previously-seen event IDs from a shared state store across two separate invocations. If the store is mocked to return 'already seen', the test does not verify that the handler actually wrote to the store on the first call and read it correctly on the second — the store's real read-after-write behaviour is required. REAL-INFRA: Not required (an in-process in-memory store or local SQLite/Redis test instance suffices). MOCK-HIDE: A mock store trivially satisfies any dedup logic; the bug could be that the handler never writes IDs, which a mock would silently hide. REJECTION-GATE: System is not required because no OS-level external dependency is needed; an in-process test store exposes the real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the handler with a real (or in-memory-backed) deduplication store. Send a valid event. Assert the queue received exactly one write. Send the identical event a second time. Assert the queue still has exactly one write total, and the second response is still 200 (acknowledged but not re-enqueued).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives entirely in the handler's error-handling branch — does it correctly translate a 'queue full' signal into a 503 with a Retry-After header? The queue itself is not under test; only the handler's response to the queue's reported state is. REAL-INFRA: Not required. MOCK-HIDE: A mock queue returning a 'full' error is exactly the right instrument because we are testing the handler's behavior, not the queue's capacity enforcement (that is behavior 7). REJECTION-GATE: Integration would be needed only if the handler's 503 path depended on state shared across modules; here it is a simple conditional on the queue call's return value.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the handler with a valid request and a mock queue whose enqueue method rejects with a 'queue full' error. Assert the response status is 503 and that the Retry-After header is present with a valid value.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior tests the queue module's own internals, not the handler. The word 'actually' signals that the real queue implementation (including its backing store) must be exercised — a mock would trivially pass by construction. The failure boundary is the interaction between the queue's enqueue logic and the persistent store it uses to count or cap items. REAL-INFRA: The queue's persistence layer (even an in-process SQLite, file-based queue, or in-memory store with a real size counter) must be real; OS-level or external network is not required. MOCK-HIDE: Any mock of the queue trivially satisfies this claim and hides the real enforcement logic. REJECTION-GATE: System would be required only if the queue relied on a real external service (Redis, RabbitMQ); the behavior does not specify this, so Integration (real queue module + local backing store) is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real queue module with a capacity of N (e.g., 3). Enqueue N items and assert all succeed. Attempt to enqueue a (N+1)th item and assert the queue rejects it with the expected 'full' error. Optionally dequeue one item and verify a subsequent enqueue succeeds, confirming the limit is dynamic, not permanent.
